reason: Both define a vertex of a graph as an element of the vertex set; 'node' is a synonym of 'vertex' (both -> 'dinh').
Identical definition: G=(V,E) with directed edges as ordered vertex pairs; 'digraph' is the abbreviation of 'directed graph' (both -> 'do thi co huong').
The arc entry defines a directed edge: an ordered pair (u,v) of a digraph; 'arc' and 'directed edge' denote the same object.
Both define an injection: f(a1)=f(a2) implies a1=a2 (both -> 'don anh').
Both define a surjection: every b in B is f(a) for some a (both -> 'toan anh').

Both define graph isomorphism via an adjacency-preserving bijection (both -> 'dang cau'); canonical kept as 'isomorphic' because an existing stub (isomorphic-simple-graphs) already redirects there, avoiding a redirect chain.
Both define two graphs as homeomorphic when obtainable from a common graph by elementary edge subdivisions (both -> 'dong phoi').
Both define a vertex whose removal disconnects a connected graph (both -> 'dinh cat').
Both define W_n as C_n plus a hub vertex joined to all cycle vertices, with n+1 vertices and 2n edges (both -> 'do thi banh xe').
Both define a graph on n vertices with no edges (both -> 'do thi rong'); the null-graph headword even glosses itself as 'empty graph'.
Both define the complement of a simple graph G as same vertex set with adjacency reversed (both -> 'do thi bu').
Both define the floor function as the greatest integer <= x (both -> 'ham san').
Both define vertex connectivity kappa(G) as the minimum number of vertices whose removal disconnects G or leaves one vertex (both -> 'do lien thong dinh').
Both define a flow as a nonnegative capacity-bounded function on arcs satisfying conservation at intermediate vertices (both -> 'luong van tai').
Both define the remainder r with 0<=r<m from dividing a by m, i.e. a mod m (both -> 'so du').
Both define the range as f(A) = {f(a): a in A}, a subset of the codomain (both -> 'tap anh').
Both define a binary relation from A to B as a subset of A x B (both -> 'quan he hai ngoi'); an existing stub (relation-on-a-set) already redirects to binary-relation.

Both define a reflexive, antisymmetric, transitive binary relation; order-relation explicitly equals partial order (both -> 'quan he thu tu'); existing stubs partial-ordering/total-ordering already redirect to *-order entries.
Both define a rooted tree with a unique directed path from the root to every other vertex; the arborescence entry is the directed-sense rooted tree (both -> 'cay co goc').
Both define deg_G(v) as the number of edges incident to v, loops counted twice (both -> 'bac cua dinh').
Both define deg^-(v) as the number of arcs with v as terminal endpoint (both -> 'bac vao').
Both define deg^+(v) as the number of arcs with v as initial endpoint (both -> 'bac ra').
Both define a digraph as strongly connected when directed paths exist both ways between every pair of vertices (both -> 'lien thong manh').

concept: terminal vertex / head of an arc
reason: Both define the head/terminal vertex v of a directed edge (u,v) (both -> 'dinh cuoi').

concept: outer (unbounded) face of a planar graph
reason: Both define the unique unbounded region of a planar embedding (both -> 'mien vo han').
Both define the set with no elements, subset of every set (both -> 'tap (hop) rong'); null-set headword glosses itself as 'empty set'.
Both define a rooted tree where every internal vertex has at most m children (both -> 'cay m-phan'); only the parameter letter differs.
All three define Q_n on 2^n binary strings of length n, adjacent iff differing in one bit (all -> 'do thi lap phuong'); only the dimension letter differs.
All three state the sum rule for disjoint counting tasks (all -> 'quy tac cong'); existing stubs sum-rule-for-counting/-for-sets already redirect to the-sum-rule.
Both state the product rule for sequential counting tasks (both -> 'quy tac nhan'); existing stubs product-rule-for-counting/-for-sets already redirect to the-product-rule.
All three state the basic pigeonhole/Dirichlet principle (more objects than boxes forces a box with two); the separate generalized form is intentionally excluded.
Both define an Euler path as a path traversing every edge exactly once (both -> 'duong di Euler'); the eulerian-path entry adds only the existence condition.

concept: trail (simple path: walk with no repeated edge)
reason: Both define a walk that uses no edge more than once (vertices may repeat) (both -> 'duong di don'); this is the trail/simple-path concept, distinct from 'path'.
Both define the height of a rooted tree as the maximum vertex level, i.e. the longest root-to-leaf path.

concept: level of a vertex in a rooted tree
reason: Both define the level of a vertex as the length of the unique root-to-vertex path, root at level 0; existing stub level-of-a-vertex already redirects to level-of-a-tree.
Both define the length of a path as the number of edges on it (both -> 'do dai (duong di)'); the length entry also covers circuits and weighted graphs as the same notion.
